root.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Integrated Case Management Guide
root.summary: Front matter and Chapter 1 of the Cúram 8.1.2 Integrated Case Management Guide. It introduces the needs-to-delivery model: assessing needs through triage, screening, and intake; creating integrated cases; delivering benefits via product delivery cases; delivering services and referrals; using the caseworker workspace; and managing ongoing cases (owner/supervisor changes, audits, notes, evidence, legal proceedings, reassessment, suspension/closure/reactivation). It also covers case security at a high level, including function, field, location, program, and sensitivity security. The section includes the table of contents and navigation content, plus legal front matter such as notices, privacy policy, trademarks, copyright, export/warranty disclaimers, and publication-use terms. Useful as an overview and entry point to the guide, not a task-level procedure.

children.title: Note
children.summary: This section is a brief front-matter note. It directs readers to read the Notices section on page 63 before using the information or the product. It also shows the copyright notice for Merative US L.P. (2012, 2024).

children.title: Edition
Edition — pages 5-14
children.summary: This section is the edition front matter for the Cúram 8.1/8.1.1/8.1.2 documentation. It identifies the product versions covered and includes copyright/trademark notices. The included contents pages preview the major manual structure: Integrated Case Management, needs-to-delivery processes, product delivery cases, service deliveries/referrals, caseworker workspace, ongoing case management, and securing cases. Useful as a navigation entry point, but it contains no procedural guidance beyond the document scope and table of contents.

children.title: Edition — pages 15-24
children.summary: This section explains Cúram integrated case management for product delivery cases: creating cases within an integrated case, selecting the primary client, capturing and sharing evidence, identifying evidence issues, checking eligibility, and moving cases through submission, approval, activation, and payment. It also covers decision views and comparison for explaining eligibility changes, and financial processing: case nominees, case components, delivery patterns, scheduling/issuing payments and bills, and case deductions.

children.title: Edition — pages 25-34
children.summary: This section covers case finance and monitoring plus service delivery/referrals. It explains payment simulations, financial transaction lists, recent case lists, related cases, case transaction logs, and product delivery status history/statuses. It then introduces service and referral workflows: creating services, checking client availability, finding and vetting providers, estimating service costs, sending enquiry/notification letters, recording provider responses, and approving service deliveries. Useful if you need details on case processing history, payment/deduction tracking, provider selection, or referral/service scheduling and approval.

children.title: Edition — pages 35-44
children.summary: Covers service delivery lifecycle and case management navigation. Explains approval/rejection rules, eligibility checks, manual approval tasks, service authorization, payments to clients/providers, and how actual service costs are updated from invoices/attendance. Also includes changing/canceling services, completing services and recording outcomes, evaluating service quality, and viewing payment/financial transactions. The later pages introduce caseworker and supervisor summaries, case calendars, case search/query tools, items of interest, and the integrated case overview/Entitlements card with program, entitlement, payment, and determination details.

children.title: Edition — pages 45-54
children.summary: Covers core case-management functions: changing case owner/supervisor and showing contextual case information for integrated and product delivery cases. Describes case audits; case calendar features for meetings, events, activities, referrals, reviews, and appeals; translation requirements; contracts; communications; attachments; and note handling, including editing, history, and Rich Text Editor allowlist configuration. Also includes certification of case evidence and tracking issues/legal proceedings, with special cautions, investigations, appeals, and legal actions.

children.title: Edition — pages 55-62
children.summary: Covers integrated case management features for issues, investigations, appeals, legal actions, and special cautions, then explains case reassessment, over/underpayment handling, and case lifecycle actions (suspend, close, reactivate). Also introduces case security: function, field, location-based, program, and sensitivity security, including how access is controlled for cases, fields, programs, services, and notes. Useful for navigation if you need operational case processes, payment correction behavior, or security configuration concepts.

Front matter and chapter 1 of Cúram Integrated Case Management (8.1/8.1.1/8.1.2). Covers the needs-to-delivery model: intake, screening, creating integrated cases, product delivery cases, service deliveries/referrals, caseworker workspace, ongoing case management, reassessment, suspending/closing/reactivating cases, and case security. Also includes TOC/navigation content. Useful as an entry point if you need the overall business process and major case-management topics; not a procedural how-to for a single task.

children.title: Notices
Privacy policy
children.summary: Contains legal and copyright notices: fictitious example data disclaimer, sample program license terms, warranty/liability limitations, a link to the Merative privacy policy, trademark acknowledgments for Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft, and UNIX, and the copyright notice (© Merative US L.P. 2012, 2024).

children.title: Trademarks
children.summary: Lists trademark and copyright notices for Merative documentation. States that sample names and data are fictitious, sample programs may be copied and modified under the Merative Client Agreement, and are provided “AS IS” without warranty. Includes the privacy policy link. Identifies trademarks for Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft/Windows, and UNIX, plus a notice that other names may be trademarks of others.

Legal notices and publication-use terms for Merative documentation. Covers personal and enterprise use permissions, preservation of proprietary notices, restrictions on derivative works and redistribution, no implied rights beyond those stated, export-law compliance, and Merative’s right to withdraw permission. Includes broad “as-is” warranty disclaimer, patent notice, third-party product/website disclaimer, and the copyright notice © Merative US L.P. 2012, 2024.

children.title: Contents
children.summary: Table of contents for the Cúram Integrated Case Management guide. Lists major sections on needs-to-delivery case management, including triage/screening/intake, product delivery cases, service delivery and referrals, workspace features, ongoing case management, and securing cases. Also shows subtopics such as eligibility, payments, scheduling, service provider selection, case calendars, audits, notes, evidence, legal proceedings, and case status changes. Useful as a navigation node to find specific features and procedures in the guide.

children.title: 1 Cúram Integrated Case Management
1.1 Needs to Delivery - Integrated Case Management
Delivering benefits through product deliveries
children.summary: This section explains how Cúram Integrated Case Management delivers benefits through product delivery cases. It describes the flow from initial client contact and screening, to assessing needs, creating product delivery cases, and determining real eligibility and entitlement for benefits. It emphasizes a holistic, long-term approach across multiple benefits and services, potentially over a client’s lifetime, and notes that delivery can include benefits and services aimed at improving client outcomes.

children.title: Meeting needs through service deliveries
children.summary: Explains how agencies meet client needs through service deliveries using product delivery processing, CPM service delivery processing, or both. Covers direct agency-provided services, third-party provider services paid by the agency, and cases where eligibility and payment determination are required. Gives examples: Mileage Reimbursement via product delivery, Home Help via third-party service delivery, Physiotherapy using both eligibility and custom-rate payment logic, and Counseling using product delivery for eligibility plus CPM for provider payment.

children.title: Meeting needs through referrals
children.summary: Explains using referrals to connect clients with third-party service providers that may better meet their needs, usually ending agency contact unless follow-up is required. Notes that referral services are part of Cúram Provider Management, which must be installed. Also situates referrals within the broader intake-to-integrated-case process, where triage, screening, and intake assess needs before creating an integrated case to manage services and programs.

Overview of Integrated Case Management’s “needs to delivery” model: agencies assess client needs from triage/screening/intake, then match them to benefits and services over potentially long periods, even a client’s lifetime. Benefits are delivered through product delivery cases that determine eligibility/entitlement and generate financial schedules. Services can be delivered directly, through third-party providers, or by combining product delivery and Cúram Provider Management. Includes examples such as unemployment benefits, mileage reimbursement, home help, physiotherapy, and counseling.

children.title: 1.2 Assessing needs - from initial contact to integrated case creation
Supporting the triage, screening, and intake processes
children.summary: Describes tools that support an agency’s triage, screening, and intake workflows. These tools help establish a person’s needs at initial contact, assess potential and actual eligibility for needs-based programs or services, and support communication through different channels. It introduces triage as a short question set used to quickly identify services, screenings, and products that may meet a client’s needs, as part of the broader move from initial contact toward integrated case creation.

children.title: Triaging clients for programs and services
children.summary: This section explains how agencies triage, screen, and intake clients for programs and services. Triage quickly identifies possible services, programs, and screenings using basic questions about core needs such as food, shelter, health, financial, education, and safety. Screening then determines potential eligibility for specific programs, using more detailed personal and household information. It also notes related intake features: Eligibility Screening, Program Intake Processes, Life Event Management, Common Intake, and Citizen Engagement.

children.title: Screening clients for programs
children.summary: Explains screening as the process for determining a person’s potential eligibility for specific programs, distinct from triage. Triage recommends services and may direct a person to screening; screening then asks more detailed questions and records personal details such as name, DOB, address, living arrangements, and family relationships to assess eligibility for benefit-based programs like Cash Assistance and Food Assistance. Also notes related intake tools: Eligibility Screening, Program Intake Processes, Life Event Management, Common Intake, and Citizen Engagement.

children.title: Program intake processes
children.summary: Explains how program intake works in Cúram for needs-based and protection-based programs. Needs-based intake typically collects application details such as income, expenses, employment, and education through online, phone, or in-person channels, then uses the evidence to assess eligibility. Protection-based intake captures reported incidents or concerns, such as child abuse allegations, for investigation. It also describes how an integrated case may be created automatically or manually, and how product delivery cases are created to deliver authorized benefits.

children.title: Creating an integrated case
children.summary: Explains how an integrated case is created, reopened, and managed in Cúram. Covers creation through intake or manual entry for a person/prospect person and household, linkage to product delivery cases, and adding services/referrals. Describes the case lifecycle, open/close/reopen behavior, and that some organizations keep cases open. Also outlines initial case ownership rules, configurable ownership strategies, and the categories of information stored and maintained on integrated cases.

Overview of how Cúram moves from initial client contact to integrated case creation. Covers triage, screening, intake, and related tools (Common Intake, Citizen Engagement, Life Event Management) for identifying needs and eligibility across channels. Explains triage vs. screening, needs-based and protection-based program intake, and how applications capture evidence. Describes creating, reopening, closing, and owning integrated cases, including automatic/manual creation, linkage to product delivery cases, and adding service deliveries/referrals.

children.title: 1.3 Delivering benefits to clients through product delivery cases
Creating a product delivery case
children.summary: Explains how product delivery cases are created within an integrated case so shared integrated-case functions, like evidence capture, can be used. Describes selecting a case member and benefit, making that member the primary client who drives eligibility and acts as default nominee for payments. Notes the primary client cannot be changed after creation, so a new case is required for a different primary client. Includes an example showing how an applicant’s family members may be part of the case while one person remains the primary applicant.

children.title: Capturing information about a client
Advisor
children.summary: The Advisor provides context-sensitive user tips and reminders, including detecting and highlighting evidence-related issues that may affect eligibility. It is rule- and text-configured to analyze recorded or known client information automatically and surface concerns with evidence, such as missing or outstanding verification.

This section explains how client information is captured as case evidence to assess eligibility and entitlement. It describes evidence sources such as certificates, letters, and medical certification, and how users manage evidence in the evidence workspace and dashboard. It also covers lists for active, in-edit, incoming, issues, and verifications, plus the role of the Advisor in automatically flagging missing or outstanding evidence that may affect eligibility.

children.title: Checking eligibility for active and in-edit evidence
children.summary: Explains checking eligibility for active and in-edit evidence in Cúram case management. Covers how evidence is captured at the integrated case level, the meaning of in-edit, active, and canceled statuses, and how in-edit changes affect eligibility only when activated. Describes checking eligibility on active evidence only versus including in-edit evidence to preview impacts. Also notes that eligibility and entitlement decisions are created from evidence, and that cases are typically validated, submitted for approval, and then activated for payment.

children.title: Approving payments and activating a case
children.summary: Explains the approval step before payment activation. Case details and eligibility decisions are validated after check eligibility and before benefits are issued. Approval checks can route a percentage of submitted cases to supervisor review. Describes submitting a case for approval when the user is satisfied with entered details; eligibility is recalculated at submission to capture a snapshot, and the case status becomes “submitted.”

children.title: Applying rules to information to make decisions
children.summary: This section explains how agencies use rules to apply legislation to a client’s circumstances when deciding eligibility and entitlement for care and protection programs. It gives the example that a person may qualify for income support only if income is below a threshold. It sits after case approval/activation content and likely introduces rule-based decision-making as a broader concept for determining benefits.

children.title: Reading decisions, eligibility, entitlement, and explanation
Viewing decisions over the lifetime of a case
Current determination and determination history views
children.summary: Describes the current determination view and determination history for eligibility decisions over a case lifecycle. Explains that the current view shows the most recent determination from initial assessment or reassessment, including reassessment completion time, last decision update time, and status messages for in-progress, pending, or deferred reassessments. Notes agencies can configure whether these informational messages display. Also mentions that key decision factors are deprecated, though graphical views previously used them to explain eligibility and entitlement decisions.

children.title: Graphical and list determination view
children.summary: Explains how determinations are viewed in Cúram, including graphical and flat list formats. The graphical view shows eligibility results, entitlement amounts, and influencing information across the case timeline, helping users understand why a person is eligible or ineligible; the key decision factors feature is deprecated. It also covers determination history for active cases, showing current and superseded determinations from assessments or reassessments. The decision details view provides tabbed, detailed information for a selected decision.

children.title: Decision details view
children.summary: Explains the Decision details view, accessed by selecting a specific decision from a determination list. It shows detailed information about an individual eligibility decision through multiple tabs to help users understand a client’s eligibility. The section sits within the broader determination history, current determination, and graphical/list determination views, and notes that users can inspect decision details for each decision in a determination, including examples such as entitlement amount and household members included in eligibility.

Explains how Cúram shows eligibility decisions across a case’s lifetime. Covers current determination and determination history views, including reassessment status, timestamps, superseded determinations, and decision summaries. Describes graphical and flat list views that plot eligibility and entitlement over time, with explanatory information; key decision factors are deprecated. Also covers the decision details view, opened from a decision list, which shows tabbed, detailed information for an individual decision and its eligibility factors.

children.title: Comparing decisions
children.summary: Explains decision comparison in Cúram: users can compare a decision in a determination with a previous one to see changes in eligibility/entitlement, causes of over/underpayments, and key value changes like income or net income. Notes that agencies must configure decision details rules for displaying comparison information. The excerpt then begins a new topic on financial scheduling, covering automatic scheduling of payments and bills for eligible activated product delivery cases, and introduces case nominees and case components, including component assignment history.

Explains how Cúram presents eligibility and entitlement decisions across a case’s lifetime. Covers current determination and determination history views, showing reassessment status, timestamps, superseded determinations, and decision summaries. Also describes graphical and flat-list determination views that plot decisions and influencing information over time, plus the decision details view for an individual decision with tabbed explanation data. Notes that entitlement display frequency is configurable and that key decision factors are deprecated, though still mentioned in graphical views.

children.title: Scheduling payments and bills - the Financial Scheduler
Case nominees and case components
children.summary: Explains case nominees and case components in product delivery cases. A case nominee is the participant who receives payments or bills for a specific case component, and each component represents a type of entitlement tied to a benefit. The section gives an example of income assistance paid weekly to the nominated client, notes that cases can have multiple components and nominees, and says component assignment history is tracked over time.

children.title: Scheduling payments and bills
children.summary: Explains how payments and bills are scheduled through financial components created when a product delivery case is activated. Covers default and alternate nominees, delivery patterns, and how component assignment determines who receives benefits, by what method, frequency, and period. Includes an example of weekly benefits paid monthly by check and notes that a nominee may have multiple active delivery patterns. Also describes open-ended financial components for cases configured to pay indefinitely and introduces payment/billing processing into instruction line items.

children.title: Payment and billing processing
children.summary: This section explains how case nominees and delivery patterns drive payment and billing. The primary client is the default nominee, but other participants can receive benefits, either per component or as the case’s default nominee. Financial components are created when a product delivery case is activated and define amount, method, frequency, and period. Delivery patterns determine how components are scheduled and may be changed per nominee. It also covers open-ended financial components and notes that processed financial components become instruction line items.

children.title: Issuing payments and bills
children.summary: This section explains how financial components become financial instruction line items and are rolled up into payment or bill instructions. It then describes issuing payments and bills for product delivery cases, either automatically during batch processing or manually by users, including front-office and one-off emergency manual payments. It also introduces case deductions, which allocate part of a benefit to liabilities or third parties, and outlines applied and unapplied deductions with examples.

children.title: Setting up case deductions
children.summary: Explains how case deductions are set up to allocate part of a benefit payment to a liability or third party. Covers three deduction types, including applied deductions that reduce benefits to repay an outstanding debt (for example, an overpayment) and unapplied deductions that withhold money for a general refund to the agency, such as recovering an emergency payment from later benefits. Useful for understanding budgeting and debt recovery from benefit payments.

children.title: Simulating payments
children.summary: Explains payment simulation in Cúram: users can preview future payments for a specific date or delivery period, including all payments, deductions, nominee details, tax adjustments, and the reduced balance per case component. Simulations can be saved, stored automatically, compared, viewed in summary/detail, or permanently removed. Also notes the system’s automatic financial transaction list, showing issued payments/bills with amounts, type, recipient, method, due date, and expanded breakdowns with deductions.

children.title: Viewing financial transactions
children.summary: This section explains the financial transactions list on cases. The system automatically tracks all payments and bills generated for eligible, active product delivery cases. The list shows transaction amount, type, nominee, payment method, and due date. Users can expand each transaction to view detailed payment or billing breakdowns by eligible case component, including deductions such as tax.

Explains the Financial Scheduler for product delivery cases: when eligibility and activation create financial components that schedule payments or bills to the primary client or nominated participants. Covers case nominees, case components, delivery patterns, open-ended components, and how schedules are processed into instruction line items and rolled up into payment/bill instructions. Also describes issuing payments and bills automatically or manually, including emergency/front-office payments, and introduces case deductions for applying benefits to liabilities or agency refunds.

children.title: Monitoring recent case transactions
Monitoring recently assigned, approved, and viewed cases
children.summary: Explains how users monitor recently assigned, approved, and viewed cases. The system maintains these lists automatically for integrated and product delivery cases, showing core details plus list-specific fields such as assignment date, previous owner, approval submitter, last transaction, start date, and status. Notes that administrators can configure retention periods and whether organization group cases appear. Also introduces viewing related cases and automatic/manual case relationships, including integrated-to-product delivery, reassessment over/underpayment links, and appeals, plus the case transaction log overview.

children.title: Viewing related cases
children.summary: Explains how to view related cases through case relationships, which can be created manually or automatically during processing. Covers automatic links between integrated and product delivery cases, between product delivery cases after reassessment (to preserve history), and between product delivery cases and appeals. Also introduces the case transaction log as an automatically maintained summary of processing for each case.

children.title: Using the case transaction log
children.summary: This section explains the case transaction log, which automatically records a summary of processing for every case and is updated when predefined processing events occur. It also covers related case navigation features: recently assigned, approved, and viewed case lists with key details and retention settings, plus how case relationships are created manually or automatically between integrated cases, product delivery cases, over/underpayment cases, and appeals.

children.title: Viewing the case status history
children.summary: Explains how product delivery case status history is maintained and used. It defines the case lifecycle stages—creation, submission, approval, activation, suspension, reactivation, and closure—and notes that status changes are shown in chronological order with the most recent first. Each entry includes the date/time and the user or system process responsible, helping users determine the exact sequence of events when multiple status changes occur on the same day.

Covers tools for monitoring recent case activity and case histories. Explains automatically maintained lists of recently assigned, approved, and viewed cases, including displayed details, retention settings, and admin options. Describes viewing related cases via manual or automatic relationships between integrated cases, product delivery cases, reassessment/overunderpayment cases, and appeals. Also explains the case transaction log, which records predefined processing events, and the case status history for product delivery cases, including lifecycle stages and chronological status changes.

Explains how product delivery cases are created within an integrated case, using a selected case member as the fixed primary client and default nominee. Covers capturing and managing evidence at the integrated-case level, including active/in-edit evidence, issues, verifications, and Advisor alerts for missing or outstanding information. Describes eligibility checks, submission for approval, approval/rejection, and activation for payment. Also introduces payment scheduling, issuing payments/bills, deductions, payment simulation, financial transaction viewing, and monitoring recent case transactions, relationships, logs, and status history.

children.title: 1.4 Delivering services to clients through service delivery and referrals
Creating services for clients
children.summary: Describes how users create services for clients: selecting an appropriate service (via taxonomy search), assigning one or more client recipients, setting service ownership, required units, frequency, participation, nominee for payments, and authorizing provider rates. Explains default behavior for single-client cases, optional duplication of a service for multiple clients, automatic owner assignment, default unit counts for some services, and that overridden provider rates require supervisor approval. Also notes checking client availability for scheduled services.

children.title: Referring clients to service providers
children.summary: Explains how Cúram handles referring clients to external, unpaid service providers. Covers choosing a referral service (registered or manual, single or multiple clients), recording the referral date, setting optional follow-up and escalation notifications, and marking when a client contacts the provider. Also describes sending notification letters to both client and provider, using templates, preferred communication methods, and communication exceptions.

children.title: Checking client schedules and availability
children.summary: This section explains how Cúram checks a client’s availability before scheduling services, referrals, or meetings. It notes that a start date is required, an end date is optional, and existing activities for the selected dates are shown to avoid conflicts. It also describes provider selection for external services/referrals, including searching by name, proximity to the client’s address, and specialty, with a map displayed for returned providers.

children.title: Finding the best service provider to meet client needs
children.summary: Explains how Cúram helps users choose the best external service provider for a client by balancing proximity, accessibility, quality, and cost. Covers searching providers by name, distance, or specialty, with maps for location context. Describes validating provider credentials through licenses, accreditations, staff background checks, incidents, and investigations. Also explains service cost estimation based on dates, authorized units, frequency, and provider-specific rate precedence, plus display of flat-rate or utilization contracts for comparison.

children.title: Enquiring about services and recording provider responses
children.summary: This section explains how agencies enquire about services from providers and record provider responses by email, web, or phone. It covers how preferred enquiry method, valid email, and provider portal access determine which method can be used; web enquiries are automatically emailed and updated in the system, while phone enquiries are recorded manually. It also notes automatic statistics on provider response time and response rate. The section then introduces service delivery approval, including supervisor review, eligibility determination, and approval for services without eligibility processing.

children.title: Approving service deliveries
children.summary: Explains the service delivery approval workflow. Services may require supervisor approval after creation, especially when a percentage of submitted services are checked, when the user lacks approval privileges, or when an authorized rate is specified. Approval can trigger eligibility determination for product-delivery services; otherwise it authorizes the client to receive the service. Rejected services return to open and must be resubmitted. It also covers submission rules, approval/rejection statuses, third-party provider payment approval, and tracking approval requests.

children.title: Paying clients and service providers
children.summary: Explains how payments are processed after service delivery approval for both clients and service providers. Covers standard product delivery vs provider management processing, including when payments are based on custom rates, fixed rates, invoices, attendance rosters, or flat-rate contracts. Describes service authorizations and line items, eligibility, and when authorizations are required. Also notes that the system tracks financial transactions and lets users view payment details for providers and clients.

children.title: Changing services
children.summary: Explains how service changes affect payment processing. Updates to recommended units, client evidence, unit amount, authorized/custom rate, or service start/end dates are automatically reflected in the relevant payment mechanism. For third-party services, changes update service authorization line items; for services using product delivery processing, start/end date changes flow through to product delivery. Also notes that service cost and delivered units are tracked automatically based on invoices, attendance rosters, and payments.

children.title: Completing services and recording outcomes
children.summary: Explains how service delivery is completed and how outcomes are recorded (successful or unsuccessful, with a reason if unsuccessful). Covers cancel behavior: canceling a service automatically cancels related authorization line items and closes any linked product delivery. Also describes service evaluations, including timing before/after completion, rating providers on configured criteria, and automatic averaging of ratings. Finally introduces the service delivery lifecycle and status history used to track progress through stages.

children.title: Evaluating the quality of services
children.summary: Explains how services are completed, with outcomes recorded as successful or unsuccessful (including reasons for failure), and how completion shows the client no longer needs the service. Also covers service quality evaluation, which can occur before or after completion, to assess provider effectiveness, help choose providers, and inform future agency decisions. Evaluations use configurable criteria with 1–5 ratings, and the system calculates an average provider rating. Begins the section on viewing service delivery status history and lifecycle status tracking.

children.title: Viewing the service delivery status history
children.summary: Explains the service delivery lifecycle and how Cúram automatically maintains each service’s status history as it moves through processing stages. Covers that each service delivery has its own status describing progress, and refers to a table defining the statuses. This section is useful for understanding how to track a service delivery’s current state and historical progression within an integrated case.

Covers the end-to-end workflow for delivering services and referrals: creating services for one or multiple clients, setting ownership, units, frequency, participation, nominees, and provider rates; checking client availability; finding and selecting providers; requesting/referring services and sending notifications; approving service deliveries; paying clients/providers; tracking changes, outcomes, evaluations, and service lifecycle status history. Includes referral follow-up/escalation and financial processing details such as authorizations, invoices, attendance rosters, flat-rate contracts, and transaction tracking.

children.title: 1.5 The caseworker workspace
Accessing caseload
children.summary: Explains how caseworkers access their caseload in the workspace. It says they can view cases directly assigned to them and cases owned by organization groups they belong to, with the list automatically maintained. Caseworkers can filter cases to show only those assigned to them or their group, depending on the case ownership strategy configured by the agency administrator. Useful for understanding caseload access and filtering behavior.

children.title: Viewing daily workload summaries
children.summary: This section explains daily workload summaries in the caseworker workspace. Caseworkers and supervisors see predefined summaries at login, giving a snapshot of work and quick access to full lists and actions. Summaries can be shown or hidden per user. Available summaries include Quick Links, My Appointments, My Tasks, My Items of Interest, and My Case Queries. It also describes what each summary shows, such as scheduled appointments, prioritized tasks, tracked items, and saved case queries.

children.title: The case calendar
children.summary: Explains the case calendar in Cúram Integrated Case Management. It helps caseworkers and clients schedule and maintain events, activities, and meetings for agency cases and clients, with monthly, weekly, and daily views. Separate calendars exist for integrated case and product delivery case levels; integrated case calendars track automatically created or manual events and support scheduling meetings. Also notes case searching and querying at user or organization level, with user searches limited to assigned cases and organization searches covering all cases in the organization.

children.title: Searching and querying cases
children.summary: This section explains case searching and querying in Cúram Integrated Case Management. It distinguishes user-level searches, which return cases assigned to the logged-in caseworker, from organization-level searches, which return any cases in the organization regardless of assignment. It also appears alongside related workload summaries and case calendar features for tracking and scheduling case events.

children.title: Recording items of interest
children.summary: This section explains how caseworkers search and track cases in Cúram. It covers searching by case/client reference number, name, status, dates, and filters for appeals, issues, investigations, and service plans. It also describes quick search by reference number across cases, participants, issues, and incidents, plus saved case queries for monitoring assigned cases. Finally, it explains recording items of interest—such as cases, issues, audit plans, appeals, and participants—and filtering that personal list by type.

The caseworker workspace helps caseworkers manage workloads and access key case information. It covers caseload access and filtering by assignment, organization group, ownership strategy, and status; daily workload summaries such as Quick Links, My Appointments, My Tasks, My Items of Interest, Caseload Summary, and My Case Queries; the case calendar for scheduling and tracking activities and meetings; user- and organization-level case search and queries; quick search by reference number; and recording/filtering items of interest like cases, issues, appeals, audit plans, and participants.

children.title: 1.6 Ongoing case management
The case overview
The Entitlements card
children.summary: Explains the Entitlements card on an integrated case Overview tab. It summarizes the latest entitlement and payment information for active and suspended programs, with configurable program lists and links to more detailed product delivery case pages. Describes expandable details including program type, start date, case status/owner, delivery schedule, entitlement and determination history, members, expected next payments (with adjustment indicators and payment impacts), previous payments, and links to payment details or over/underpayments. Useful for understanding case financial/entitlement summary behavior.

Explains the integrated case Overview tab and its Entitlements card, which summarizes the latest entitlement and payment information for active and suspended programs. Covers configurable program lists, links to product delivery case pages, and expandable details such as program type, start date, case status/owner, delivery schedule, entitlement history, members, next and previous payments, payment adjustment indicators, and links to payment details or over/underpayments. Useful for understanding case financial and entitlement summary behavior.

children.title: Changing the case owner and case supervisor
children.summary: Explains how case ownership and supervision can be changed from the system-determined defaults. A case owner may be reassigned to a user, organization unit, position, or work queue, giving shared visibility and workload flexibility. The case supervisor may be explicitly set or automatically derived from the owner’s manager, and is used during approval submission. The section also introduces contextual case information shown throughout the application, including primary client details, case status, owner, special cautions, issues/legal proceedings, and integrated-case family member views.

children.title: Viewing contextual information
children.summary: Explains how case ownership and supervision can be changed, including assigning a case to users, organization units, positions, or work queues for shared visibility and workload coverage. Describes contextual information shown for cases across the application: primary client name/photo, status, owner, special cautions, and issues or legal proceedings. For integrated cases, it also shows added family members and their relationship to the primary client, in list or photo view with age displayed.

children.title: Auditing cases
children.summary: This section defines case audits as third-party or agency evaluations of cases to assess performance and improve policy and practice. It gives an example of a Quality Assurance Review audit focused on Food Assistance delivery, including timeliness of actions and verifications, and points readers to the Case Audits Guide for more detail.

children.title: Schedule meetings and track case events with the case calendar
Scheduling meetings and recording meeting minutes
children.summary: This section explains how to schedule meetings from a case calendar and record meeting minutes. It covers meeting features such as inviting multiple participants, email/calendar integration, attendee accept/decline, rescheduling, and cancellation. It also describes the information captured in meeting minutes, including subject, location, times, organizer, notes, decisions, attendance, actions, files, and summaries, plus issuing minutes to attendees and interested parties.

children.title: Scheduling activities
children.summary: This section explains case activities as scheduled events tied to a case. It defines two activity types: standard activities, which occur once for a specific time period, and recurring activities, which repeat over time. Examples include a one-off meeting or a weekly supervised visit.

children.title: Referring clients for services
children.summary: This section explains how referrals are maintained from the case calendar for product delivery and integrated cases. A referral is a request to a service supplier for consultation, such as scheduling a medical examination. It notes that case-calendar referrals are available only when Cúram Provider Management is not installed, since Provider Management offers enhanced referral capabilities. The surrounding page also briefly distinguishes activities from meetings, and introduces case reviews, appeals, and system-generated events.

children.title: Setting up case reviews
children.summary: This section explains case review setup in the case calendar. A case review is an examination of case details by another user, created manually or automatically. Reviews are assigned to a user with the case reviewer role and sent by task notification. Automatic reviews can be configured at set frequencies, such as every 6 months, and adjusted per case.

children.title: Appealing services
children.summary: Explains how to create appeals from a product delivery case calendar when Cúram Appeals is not installed. Appeals are used to manage disputes against service providers delivering services to clients. The appeal records the service supplier involved, the appeal type (for example, disqualification conditions or payment rate), and optionally the outcome when complete. Also notes that Appeals offers more advanced handling of client requests to appeal case decisions.

children.title: System-generated events
children.summary: This section introduces system-generated events in case processing and notes that they can be created automatically by the system during specific actions. It follows discussion of calendar-based case activities, referrals, case reviews, and appeals, and indicates that the next table lists the available event types. Useful for finding which case events are automatically created and when.

This section explains the case calendar for scheduling meetings and tracking case events. It covers meetings, meeting minutes, and meeting features such as invitations, email/calendar integration, accept/decline, rescheduling, cancellation, and issued minutes. It also summarizes case activities (standard and recurring), referrals, case reviews, appeals, and system-generated events. Useful details include which items are manually vs automatically created, when referrals/appeals are available, and how calendar entries support case and participant coordination.

children.title: Case tasks
children.summary: Defines case tasks as work items that can be created manually or by the system and managed in a user’s workspace/workflow, including from a case’s task list. Gives the example of a task to approve a case submitted for approval, appearing in both the user inbox and case task list. The surrounding page also lists system-generated case events such as submit for approval, review, approval, rejection, payment, closure, pending closure, and reactivation.

children.title: Ongoing client management
Managing case members and participants
children.summary: Explains how integrated cases manage case members and participants. Case members are registered family members added to determine eligibility, while other related people and entities are case participants with roles such as primary client, correspondent, nominee, and contacts. Describes adding family members from recorded relationships, multi-role participants, and case groups like Benefit and Financial groups. Also covers how translator need is determined for cases based on a client’s preferred language, either manually or automatically by configuration and reassessed during case changes.

children.title: Determining the need for a translator
children.summary: Explains how the system determines whether a case needs a translator. Translation may be required when users cannot communicate with clients in the client’s preferred language, which is recorded at registration. The need can be set manually or automatically depending on case-type configuration, and is reassessed during case events such as adding a member or reassigning case ownership. Useful for understanding when and how translation services are triggered for an integrated case.

children.title: Maintaining client contracts
children.summary: This section explains maintaining client contracts in integrated cases. Contracts can be created for any case member except a prospect person, who must first be registered as a person. Contracts apply only to integrated cases and record a member’s responsibilities under written or verbal terms. The page also briefly mentions related case features: communication handling, automatic correspondence role assignment, and adding attachments, including supported file types and optional content management system integration.

children.title: Maintaining communications
children.summary: This section explains communications in integrated case management: a communication is correspondence to or from the agency, and communications created from a case communication list automatically relate to that case. It covers paper, telephone, and email communications, including support for Word/XSL templates and email servers. The case participant who corresponds is assigned the correspondent role automatically. It also notes where to find more detailed guidance in the Communications Overview Guide.

children.title: Adding attachments
children.summary: Explains case attachments in Cúram Integrated Case Management: attachments are supplemental files added to a case, such as scanned supporting documents (birth certificates, bank statements, contracts, invoices, pay slips). Supported types include Word, Excel, and PDF. File size is not restricted by the system, though agencies may set a limit via application properties. Access is controlled by security privileges. It also notes optional integration with a content management system, where files and related metadata can be stored and retrieved externally.

Covers ongoing client management in integrated cases: managing case members and participants, including family members, multi-role participants, and case groups; determining and updating translator need based on a client’s preferred language and case configuration; maintaining client contracts for case members except prospect persons; handling case communications from case lists with automatic correspondent assignment; and adding attachments such as scanned documents, with supported file types, security controls, and optional content management integration.

children.title: Recording and updating notes
Enabling configuration of a security allowlist for the Rich Text Editor
children.summary: Explains how to configure the Rich Text Editor security allowlist for notes pasted from external sources. The default allowlist supports only tags needed for standard editor features; administrators can customize it to permit additional HTML tags/attributes or disable filtering. The section notes that unrecognized tags/content are removed, an alert warns users when this happens, and the allowlist is defined in the Resource Store’s whitelist.properties file using tag=attributes entries.

This section explains how notes are recorded, appended, edited, and tracked in history. It covers rich text notes, sensitivity/priority, editable periods, author vs non-author permissions, pencil icons, and how note history stores versions and display order. It also describes how to configure the Rich Text Editor security allowlist for pasted content via Resource Store’s whitelist.properties, including default filtering, custom tag/attribute allowlists, and alerts when formatting or content is removed.

children.title: Certifying case evidence
children.summary: Explains case evidence certification for benefit product delivery cases: certifications validate evidence over a time period and ensure only certified periods are paid. Evidence outside the certified period is not validated until certified. Notes that certification can be a default prerequisite for case eligibility, and without required certification a case cannot be activated. Also introduces tracking of issues and legal proceedings for case participants, including issues, investigations, appeals, legal actions, and special cautions.

children.title: Tracking issues and legal proceedings
children.summary: This section explains how agencies track and manage issues and legal proceedings related to case participants. It covers issues for resolving discrepancies in eligibility determinations, investigations for allegations such as suspected fraud, and appeals against decisions on product delivery, assessment delivery, or prior appeals. It also introduces the Cúram Appeals module and its main appeal types, including hearing case appeals, hearing review appeals, and judicial review appeals.

children.title: Reassessing case eligibility and entitlement
children.summary: Explains how Cúram case reassessment updates eligibility and entitlement when evidence changes, either automatically or manually. A new determination and decisions may be created when eligibility, entitlement, or even decision details change, with timestamps shown for reassessment and decision updates. It also covers configurable informational messages, reassessment lockout while processing, options for closed cases or conditional prevention, and links reassessment to overpayment/underpayment checks by comparing new decisions with processed payments or bills.

children.title: Suspending, closing, or reactivating cases
children.summary: Explains case lifecycle actions for product delivery cases: suspending, closing, and reactivating. Covers when suspension is used, how payment behavior is controlled by configuration, and that suspended cases can be unsuspended back to open after approval/activation. Describes automatic or manual closure, reassessment and final determination on closure, payment handling, pending closure/grace period behavior, task-related closure restrictions, and reactivating closed or pending-closure cases, which return to open and must be approved and activated before benefits resume.

Covers ongoing integrated case management: the Overview tab and Entitlements card for summary entitlement/payment data, program details, payment history, over/underpayment links, and navigation to product delivery cases. Explains changing case owner/supervisor and displaying contextual case information such as client, members, cautions, issues, and appeals. Also introduces case audits, the case calendar, case tasks, and ongoing client management topics like participants, translators, contracts, communications, attachments, notes, evidence certification, issue/legal tracking, reassessment, over/underpayments, and case suspension, closure, and reactivation.

children.title: 1.7 Securing cases
children.summary: Explains how case security controls access to case and client information in Cúram. Covers multiple security levels: function security for general case maintenance, field security for protecting specific fields, location-based security for access by user/case location, program security for specific program maintenance, and sensitivity security for sensitive case notes, services, and outcome plans. Also notes the security hierarchy (role, group, identifiers, function identifiers) and that these settings determine what users can view, create, approve, or change.

Overview of Cúram Integrated Case Management and its needs-to-delivery model. Explains how agencies assess needs via triage, screening, and intake, then create integrated cases to manage benefits and services over time. Covers product delivery cases for eligibility/entitlement and financial scheduling, and service delivery/referrals for agency- or provider-delivered services using CPM. Also introduces the caseworker workspace, ongoing case management functions, and case security controls (function, field, location, program, and sensitivity security).
